author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew IX. 9.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And as Jesus passed forth from thence, He saw a man sitting at the receipt of custom,named Matthew; and He saith unto him, Follow me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when He had performed the miracle, He did not remain, lest, being in sight, He should kindle their jealousy the more; but He indulges them by retiring, and soothing their passion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then let us also do, not encountering them that are plotting against us; let us rather soothe their wound, giving way and relaxing their vehemence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore did He not call him together with Peter and John and the rest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As in their case He had come at that time, when He knew the men would obey Him; so Matthew also He then called when He was assured he would yield himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And therefore Paul again He took, as a fisher his prey, after the resurrection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because He who is acquainted with the hearts, and knows the secrets of each man’s mind, knew also when each of these would obey.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore not at the beginning did He call him, when he was yet in rather a hardened state, but after His countless miracles, and the great fame concerning Him, when He knew him to have actually become more prepared for obedience.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And we have cause also to admire the self-denialof the evangelist, how he disguises not his own former life, but adds even his name, when the others had concealed him under another appellation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why did he say he was “sitting at the receipt of custom?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To indicate the power of Him that called him, that it was not when he had left off or forsaken this wicked trade, but from the midst of the evils He drew him up; much as He converted the blessed Paul also when frantic and raging, and darting fire; which thing he himself makes a proof of the power of Him that called him, saying to the Galatians, “Ye have heard of my conversation in time past in the Jews’ religion, how that beyond measure I persecuted the church of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the fishermen too He called when they were in the midst of their business.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that was a craft not indeed in bad report, but of men rather rudely bred, not mingling with others, and endowed with great simplicity; whereas the pursuit now in question was one full of all insolence and boldness, and a mode of gain whereof no fair account could be given, a shameless traffic, a robbery under cloak of law: yet nevertheless He who uttered the call was ashamed of none of these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why talk I of His not being ashamed of a publican? since even with regard to a harlot woman, so far from being ashamed to call her, He actually permitted her to kiss His feet, and to moisten them with her tears.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for to this end He came, not to cure bodies only, but to heal likewise the wickedness of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which He did also in the case of the paralytic; and having shown clearly that He is able to forgive sins, then, not before, He comes to him whom we are now speaking of; that they might no more be troubled at seeing a publican chosen into the choir of the disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He that hath power to undo all our offenses, why marvel if He even make this man an apostle?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as thou hast seen the power of Him that called, so consider also the obedience of him that was called: how he neither resisted, nor disputing said, “What is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not indeed a deceitful calling, wherewith He calls me, being such as I am?” nay; for this humility again had been out of season: but he obeyed straightway, and did not even request to go home, and to communicate with his relations concerning this matter; as neither indeed did the fishermen; but as they left their net and their ship and their father, so did he his receipt of custom and his gain, and followed, exhibiting a mind prepared for all things; and breaking himself at once away from all worldly things, by his complete obedience he bare witness that He who called him had chosen a good time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore can it be, one may say, that he hath not told us of the others also, how and in what manner they were called; but only of Peter and James, and John and Philip, and nowhere of the others?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because these more than others were in so strange and mean ways of life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there is nothing either worse than the publican’s business, or more ordinary than fishing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that Philip also was among the very ignoble, is manifest from his country.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore these especially they proclaim to us, with their ways of life, to show that we ought to believe them in the glorious parts of their histories also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they who choose not to pass by any of the things which are accounted reproachful, but are exact in publishing these more than the rest, whether they relate to the Teacher or to the disciples; how can they be suspected in the parts which claim reverence? more especially since many signs and miracles are passed over by them, while the events of the cross, accounted to be reproaches, they utter with exact care and loudly; and the disciples’ pursuits too, and their faults, and those of their Master’s ancestry who were notorious for sins,they discover with a clear voice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence it is manifest that they made much account of truth, and wrote nothing for favor, nor for display.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having therefore called him, He also honored him with a very great honor by partaking straightway of his table; for in this way He would both give him good hope for the future, and lead him on to a greater confidence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not in a long time, but at once, He healed his vice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not with him only doth He sit down to meat, but with many others also; although this very thing was accounted a charge against Him, that He chased not away the sinners.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But neither do they conceal this point, what sort of blame is endeavored to be fixed on His proceedings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now the publicans come together as to one of the same trade; for he, exultingin the entrance of Christ, had called them all together.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The fact is, Christ used to try every kind of treatment; and not when discoursing only, nor when healing, nor when reproving His enemies, but even at His morning meal, He would often correct such as were in a bad way; hereby teaching us, that every season and every work may by possibility afford us profit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet surely what was then set before them came of injustice and covetousness; but Christ refused not to partake of it, because the ensuing gain was to be great: yea rather He becomes partaker of the same roof and table with them that have committed such offenses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such is the quality of a physician; unless he endure the corruption of the sick, he frees them not from their infirmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet undoubtedly He incurred hence an evil report: first by eating with him, then in Matthew’s house, and thirdly, in company with many publicans.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See at least how they reproach Him with this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Behold a man gluttonous, and a wine-bibber, a friend of publicans and sinners.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let them hear, as many as are striving to deck themselves with great honor for fasting, and let them consider that our Lord was called “a man gluttonous and a winebibber,” and He was not ashamed, but overlooked all these things, that he might accomplish what He had set before him; which indeed was accordingly done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the publican was actually converted, and thus became a better man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to teach thee that this great thing was wrought by his partaking of the table with Him, hear what Zacchæus saith, another publican.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, when he heard Christ saying, “To-day, I must abide in thy house,” the delight gave him wings, and he saith, “The half of my goods I give to the poor, and if I have taken anything from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to him Jesus saith, “This day is salvation come to this house.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So possible is it by all ways to give instruction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But how is it, one may say, that Paul commands, “If any man that is called a brother be a fornicator or covetous, with such an one no, not to eat?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the first place, it is not as yet manifest, whether to teachers also he gives this charge, and not rather to brethren only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Next, these were not yet of the number of the perfect,nor of those who had become brethren.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, Paul commands, even with respect to them that had become brethren, then to shrink from them, when they continue as they were, but these had now ceased, and were converted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But none of these things shamed the Pharisees, but they accuse Him to His disciples, saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why eateth your Master with publicans and sinners?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when the disciples seem to be doing wrong, they intercede with Him, saying, “Behold thy disciples do that which is not lawful to do on the sabbath-day;”but here to them they discredit Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All which was the part of men dealing craftily, and wishing to separate from the Master the choir of the disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Infinite Wisdom?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “They that be whole need not a physician,” saith He, “but they that are sick.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See how He turned their reasoning to the opposite conclusion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, while they made it a charge against Him that He was in company with these men: He on the contrary saith, that His not being with them would be unworthy of Him, and of His love of man; and that to amend such persons is not only blameless, but excellent, and necessary, and deserving of all sorts of praise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, that He might not seem to put them that were bidden to shame, by saying, “they that are sick;” see how He makes up for it again, by reproving the others, and saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Go ye and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now this He said, to upbraid them with their ignorance of the Scriptures.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He orders His discourse more sharply, not Himself in anger, far from it; but so as that the publicans might not be in utter perplexity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet of course He might say, “Did ye not mark, how I remitted the sins of the sick of the palsy, how I braced up his body?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He saith no such thing, but argues with them first from men’s common reasonings, and then from the Scriptures.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For having said, “They that be whole need not a physician, but they that are sick;” and having covertly indicated that He Himself was the Physician; after that He said, “Go ye and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus doth Paul also: when he had first established his reasoning by illustrations from common things, and had said, “Who feedeth a flock, and eateth not of the milk thereof?”then he brings in the Scriptures also, saying, “It is written in the law of Moses, Thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn;”and again, “Even so hath the Lord ordained, that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But to His disciples not so, but He puts them in mind of His signs, saying on this wise, “Do ye not yet remember the five loaves of the five thousand, and how many baskets ye took up?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not so however with these, but He reminds them of our common infirmity, and signifies them at any rate to be of the number of the infirm; who did not so much as know the Scriptures, but making light of the rest of virtue, laid all the stress on their sacrifices; which thing He is also earnestly intimating unto them, when He sets down in brief what had been affirmed by all the prophets,saying, “Learn ye what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The fact is, He is signifying hereby that not He was transgressing the law, but they; as if He had said, “Wherefore accuse me?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because I bring sinners to amendment?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then ye must accuse the Father also for this.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Much as He said also elsewhere, establishing this point: “My Father worketh hitherto, and I work:”so here again, “Go ye and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For as this is His will, saith Christ, so also mine.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how the one is superfluous, the other necessary?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither did He say, “I will have mercy, and sacrifice,” but, “I will have mercy, and not sacrifice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, the one thing He allowed, the other He cast out; and proved that what they blamed, so far from being forbidden, was even ordained by the law, and more so than sacrifice; and He brings in the Old Testament, speaking words and ordaining laws in harmony with Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having then reproved them, both by common illustrations and by the Scriptures, He adds again,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “I am not come to call righteous men, but sinners to repentance.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He saith unto them in irony; as when He said, “Behold, Adam is become as one of us;”and again, “If I were hungry, I would not tell thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that no man on earth was righteous, Paul declared, saying, “For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And by this too the others were comforted, I mean, the guests.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why, I am so far,” saith He, “from loathing sinners, that even for their sakes only am I come.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, lest He should make them more careless, He staid not at the word “sinners,” but added, “unto repentance.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For I am not come that they should continue sinners, but that they should alter, and amend.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He then having stopped their mouths every way, as well from the Scriptures as from the natural consequence of things; and they having nothing to say, proved as they were obnoxious to the charges which they had brought against Him, and adversaries of the law and the Old Testament; they leave Him, and again transfer their accusation to the disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Luke indeed affirms that the Pharisees said it, but this evangelist, that it was the disciples of John;but it is likely that both said it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, they being, as might be expected, in utter perplexity, take the other sort with them; as they did afterwards with the Herodians likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since in truth John’s disciples were always disposed to be jealous of Him, and reasoned against Him: being then only humbled, when first John abode in the prison.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They came at least then, “and told Jesus;”but afterwards they returned to their former envy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now what say they?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why do we and the Pharisees fast oft, but thy disciples fast not?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This is the disease, which Christ long before was eradicating, in the words, “When thou fastest, anoint thy head, and wash thy face;”foreknowing the evils that spring therefrom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But yet He doth not rebuke even these, nor say, “O ye vainglorious and over-busy;” but He discourses to them with all gentleness, saying, “The children of the bride-chamber cannot fast, as long as the bridegroom is with them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, when others were to be spoken for, the publicans I mean, to soothe their wounded soul, He was more severe in His reproof of their revilers; but when they were deriding Himself and His disciples, He makes His reply with all gentleness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now their meaning is like this; “Granted,” say they, “Thou doest this as a physician; why do Thy disciples also leave fasting, and cleave to such tables?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, to make the accusation heavier, they put themselves first, and then the Pharisees; wishing by the comparison to aggravate the charge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed “both we,” it is said, “and the Pharisees, fast oft.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in truth they did fast, the one having learnt it from John, the other from the law; even as also the Pharisee said, “I fast twice in the week.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Jesus?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Can the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Before, He called Himself a physician, but here a bridegroom; by these names revealing His unspeakable mysteries.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet of course He might have told them, more sharply, “These things depend not on you, that you should make such laws.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of what use is fasting, when the mind is full of wickedness; when ye blame others, when ye condemn them, bearing about beams in your eyes, and do all for display?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, before all this ye ought to have cast out vainglory, to be proficients in all the other duties, in charity, meekness, brotherly love.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: However, nothing of this kind doth He say, but with all gentleness, “The children of the bridechamber cannot fast, so long as the bridegroom is with them;” recalling to their mind John’s words, when he said, “He that hath the bride, is the bridegroom, but the friend of the bridegroom, which standeth and heareth Him, rejoiceth greatly because of the bridegroom’s voice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now His meaning is like this: The present time is of joy and gladness, therefore do not bring in the things which are melancholy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For fasting is a melancholy thing, not in its own nature, but to them that are yet in rather a feeble state; for to those at least that are willing to practise self-command, the observance is exceedingly pleasant and desirable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as when the body is in health, the spirits are high,so when the soul is well conditioned, the pleasure is greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But according to their previous impression He saith this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So also Isaiah,discoursing of it, calls it “an affliction of the soul;” and Moses too in like manner.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not however by this only doth He stop their mouths, but by another topic also, saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken from them, and then shall they fast.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For hereby He signifies, that what they did was not of gluttony, but pertained to some marvellous dispensation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And at the same time He lays beforehand the foundation of what He was to say touching His passion, in His controversies with others instructing His disciples, and training them now to be versed in the things which are deemed sorrowful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because for themselves already to have this said to them, would have been grievous and galling, since we know that afterwards, being uttered, it troubled them;but spoken to others, it would become rather less intolerable to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It being also natural for them to pride themselves on John’s calamity, He from this topic represses likewise such their elation: the doctrine however of His resurrection He adds not yet, it not being yet time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so much indeed was natural, that one supposed to be a man should die, but that other was beyond nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then what He had done before, this He doth here again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, that as He, when they were attempting to prove Him blameable for eating with sinners, proved to them on the contrary, that His proceeding was not only no blame, but an absolute praise to Him: so here too, when they wanted to show of Him, that He knows not how to manage His disciples, He signifies that such language was the part of men not knowing how to manage their inferences,but finding fault at random.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For no man,” saith He, “putteth a piece of new cloth unto an old garment.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He is again establishing His argument by illustrations from common life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what He saith is like this, “The disciples have not yet become strong, but still need much condescension.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They have not yet been renewed by the Spirit, and on persons in that state one ought not to lay any burden of injunctions.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things He said, setting laws and rules for His own disciples, that when they should have to receive as disciples those of all sorts that should come from the whole world, they might deal with them very gently.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Neither do men put new wine into old bottles.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou His illustrations, how like the Old Testament? the garment? the wine skins?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For Jeremiah too calls the people “a girdle,” and makes mention again of “bottles” and of “wine.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, the discourse being about gluttony and a table, He takes His illustrations from the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Lukethe same words, a second and a third time and often; not however in a wearisome kind of way, but sport ively, and do thou now turn from her, now flatter and court her.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not the painters, how much they rub out, how much they insert, when they are making a beautiful portrait?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well then, do not thou prove inferior to these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if these, in drawing the likeness of a body, used such great diligence, how much more were it meet for us, in fashioning a soul, to use every contrivance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou shouldest fashion well the form of this soul, thou wilt not see the countenance of the body looking unseemly, nor lips stained, nor a mouth like a bear’s mouth dyed with blood, nor eyebrows blackened as with the smut of some kitchen vessel, nor cheeks whitened with dust like the walls of the tombs.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For all these things are smut, and cinders, and dust, and signals of extreme deformity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But stay: I have been led on unobserving, I know not how, into these expressions; and while admonishing another to teach with gentleness, I have been myself hurried awayinto wrath.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us return therefore again unto the more gentle way of admonition, and let us bear with all the faults of our wives, that we may succeed in doing what we would.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not how we bear with the cries of children, when we would wean them from the breast, how we endure all for this object only, that we may persuade them to despise their former food?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus let us do in this case also, let us bear with all the rest, that we may accomplish this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when this hath been amended, thou wilt see the other too proceeding in due order, and thou wilt come again unto the ornaments of gold, and in the same way wilt reason concerning them likewise, and thus by little and little bringing thy wife unto the right rule, thou wilt be a beautiful painter, a faithful servant, an excellent husbandman.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Together with these things remind her also of the women of old, of Sarah, of Rebecca, both of the fair and of them that were not so, and point out how all equally practised modesty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even Leah, the wife of the patriarch, not being fair, was not constrained to devise any such thing, but although she were uncomely, and not very much beloved by her husband, she neither devised any such thing, nor marred her countenance, but continued to preserve the lineaments thereof undisfigured, and this though brought up by Gentiles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou that art a believing woman, thou that hast Christ for thine head, art thou bringing in upon us a satanic art?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And dost thou not call to mind the water that dashed over thy countenance, the sacrifice that adorns thy lips, the blood that hath reddened thy tongue?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou wouldest consider all these things, though thou wert fond of dress to the ten thousandth degree, thou wilt not venture nor endure to put upon thee that dust and those cinders.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Learn that thou hast been joined unto Christ, and refrain from this unseemliness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither is He delighted with these colorings, but He seeks after another beauty, of which He is in an exceeding degree a lover, I mean, that in the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This the prophet likewise hath charged thee to cherish, and hath said, “So shall the King have pleasure in thy beauty.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore be curious in making ourselves unseemly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither is any one of God’s works imperfect, nor doth it need to be set right by thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not even if to an image of the emperor, after it was set up, any one were to seek to add his own work, would the attempt be safe, but he will incur extreme danger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well then, man works and thou addest not; but doth God work, and dost thou amend it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And dost thou not consider the fire of hell?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dost thou not consider the destitution of thy soul?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For on this account it is neglected, because all thy care is wasted on the flesh.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why do I speak of the soul?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to the very flesh everything falls out contrary to what ye have sought.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dost thou wish to appear beautiful?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This shows thee uncomely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dost thou wish to please thy husband?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This rather grieves him; and causes not him only, but strangers also, to become thine accusers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wouldest thou appear young?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This will quickly bring thee to old age.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wouldest thou wish to array thyself honorably?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This makes thee to be ashamed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such an one is ashamed not only before those of her own rank, but even those of her maids who are in her secret, and those of her servants who know; and, above all, before herself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why need I say these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that which is more grievous than all I have now omitted, namely, that thou dost offend God; thou underminest modesty, kindlest the flame of jealousy, emulatest the harlot women at their brothel.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All these things then consider, ye women, and laugh to scorn the pomp of Satan and the craft of the devil; and letting go this adorning, or rather disfiguring, cultivate that beauty in your own souls which is lovely even to angels and desired of God, and delightful to your husbands; that ye may attain both unto present glory, and unto that which is to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To which God grant that we may all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might forever and ever.
